text: The philosopher Michael Polanyi argued that we know more than we can tell — that expert knowledge contains a tacit dimension inaccessible to explicit description. A master chess player cannot fully articulate why a position feels dangerous; a surgeon cannot enumerate every microadjustment that constitutes surgical touch. This tacit knowledge accumulates only through prolonged engagement with a domain, not through reading about it. Newport draws the implication: skills whose value rests on tacit knowledge are structurally resistant to imitation. They require the very thing that distraction culture makes scarce — extended, repeated immersion in a single domain over years.
question: What property of tacit knowledge, according to the passage, makes it resistant to imitation?

options.text: It is protected by intellectual property law and professional licensing requirements
It can only be acquired through prolonged, repeated immersion and cannot be fully articulated
It requires specialized equipment that limits access to a small number of practitioners
It decays rapidly in others who lack the same innate cognitive architecture
